title: DP on trees: Tree diameter with weights (DP style).
description: DP on trees: Tree diameter with weights (DP style).

examples.explanation: Tree: 0-1-2. Distances: (0,1)=5, (0,2)=15 (0-1-2: 5+10), (1,2)=10. Maximum is 15 between nodes 0 and 2.

approaches.name: Brute Force O(n²) via All Paths
approaches.summary: Compute the distance for every pair of nodes in the tree by exploring all paths, typically using DFS from each node. This is straightforward but inefficient for large trees.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Tree Diameter via All-Pairs DFS
ladders.desc: Compute the diameter of a weighted tree by calculating distances between all node pairs using DFS from each node.
ladders.explanation: The tree diameter is defined as the longest path between any two nodes in the tree, where path length is the sum of edge weights along that path. In a brute-force approach, we can find this by considering every possible pair of nodes (u, v) and computing the distance between them. Since the tree has n nodes, there are n(n-1)/2 pairs. For each pair, we perform a DFS to find the path distance. The intuition is straightforward: by checking all pairs, we guarantee finding the maximum distance. However, this is inefficient for large trees due to O(n²) time complexity. The algorithm works as follows: 1) Represent the tree as an adjacency list with edge weights. 2) For each node u from 0 to n-1, perform DFS to compute distances to all other nodes v. 3) During DFS from u, maintain a visited set and recursively accumulate distances. 4) Track the maximum distance encountered across all pairs. Time complexity is O(n²) because we run DFS (O(n)) from each of n nodes. Space complexity is O(n) for recursion stack and adjacency list. This connects to the overall approach by implementing the complete brute-force solution. Common mistakes include: forgetting to handle weighted edges (using 1 instead of actual weights), not resetting visited array between DFS calls, and inefficiently recomputing distances. Real code pattern: def tree_diameter_bruteforce(n, edges): adjacency = build_adjacency(n, edges); max_diameter = 0; for u in range(n): visited = [False]*n; stack = [(u, 0)]; while stack: node, dist = stack.pop(); if not visited[node]: visited[node] = True; max_diameter = max(max_diameter, dist); for neighbor, weight in adjacency[node]: if not visited[neighbor]: stack.append((neighbor, dist + weight)); return max_diameter.

examples.trace: Tree: 0-1-2. Distances: (0,1)=5, (0,2)=15 (0-1-2: 5+10), (1,2)=10. Maximum is 15 between nodes 0 and 2.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — DFS from a Single Source
ladders.desc: Perform DFS from a given node to compute distances to all other nodes in a weighted tree.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine is the core of the brute-force approach: from a starting node u, we traverse the entire tree using DFS to find the distance to every other node v. The intuition is that in a tree, there is exactly one unique path between any two nodes, so DFS will naturally follow that path and accumulate edge weights. The algorithm: 1) Initialize a visited array of size n set to False. 2) Use a stack (or recursion) starting with (u, distance=0). 3) While stack is not empty, pop a node and its current distance. If not visited, mark it visited, record the distance, and push all unvisited neighbors with updated distances (current distance + edge weight). 4) Continue until all nodes are visited. This computes distances from u to all nodes. Time complexity is O(n) for a single DFS since we visit each node once. Space complexity is O(n) for stack and visited array. This feeds into L0 by being called for each node u to get all-pairs distances. Common mistakes: not using weighted edges in distance accumulation, infinite recursion due to not marking nodes visited before pushing neighbors, and using BFS incorrectly for weighted edges. Real code pattern: def dfs_from_source(u, n, adjacency): visited = [False]*n; distances = [0]*n; stack = [(u, 0)]; while stack: node, dist = stack.pop(); if not visited[node]: visited[node] = True; distances[node] = dist; for neighbor, weight in adjacency[node]: if not visited[neighbor]: stack.append((neighbor, dist + weight)); return distances.

examples.trace: Start at node 0 with distance 0. Visit 0: dist=0. Push neighbor 1 with dist=0+2=2. Pop 1: dist=2. Push neighbors: 2 with dist=2+3=5, 3 with dist=2+1=3. Pop 3: dist=3. Pop 2: dist=5. Distances: [0,2,5,3].
Start at node 1 with distance 0. Visit 1: dist=0. Push neighbors: 0 with dist=0+5=5, 2 with dist=0+10=10. Pop 2: dist=10. Pop 0: dist=5. Distances: [5,0,10].

ladders.title: Core Logic — Computing Distance Between Two Nodes in a Tree
ladders.desc: Find the unique path distance between two specific nodes in a weighted tree using DFS.
ladders.explanation: This is the central technique: given two nodes u and v in a tree, compute the sum of edge weights along the unique path connecting them. The intuition relies on tree properties: there are no cycles, so exactly one path exists between any two nodes. We can use DFS from u to search for v, accumulating weights as we traverse. The algorithm: 1) Perform DFS from u, tracking current distance. 2) Stop when we reach v and return the accumulated distance. 3) If using recursion, backtrack if v is not found in a subtree. This works because DFS explores all paths from u, and since there's only one path to v, it will be found. Time complexity is O(n) in worst case if the tree is a line. Space complexity is O(n) for recursion stack. This feeds into L1 by being the fundamental operation within DFS from a source to compute distances to multiple nodes. Common mistakes: not handling weighted edges correctly, using BFS which works but is less intuitive for accumulation, and not marking visited nodes to avoid cycles (though trees are acyclic, it's good practice). Real code pattern: def distance_between(u, v, n, adjacency): visited = [False]*n; def dfs(node, current_dist): if node == v: return current_dist; visited[node] = True; for neighbor, weight in adjacency[node]: if not visited[neighbor]: result = dfs(neighbor, current_dist + weight); if result != -1: return result; return -1; return dfs(u, 0).

examples.trace: Start at node 0 with dist=0. Move to neighbor 1: dist=0+2=2. At node 1, check neighbors: move to 2: dist=2+3=5. Found v=2, return 5.
Start at node 2 with dist=0. Move to neighbor 1: dist=0+10=10. At node 1, move to neighbor 0: dist=10+5=15. Found v=0, return 15.

ladders.title: Building Block — Traversing a Tree with DFS
ladders.desc: Perform depth-first search on a tree to visit all nodes, handling edge weights.
ladders.explanation: This intermediate concept involves traversing a tree from a starting node using DFS, which is essential for exploring paths. The intuition is that DFS goes deep into one branch before backtracking, which naturally follows paths in a tree. In a weighted tree, we also track the cumulative distance from the start. The algorithm: 1) Start at a root node. 2) Use a stack (iterative) or recursion to visit nodes. 3) For each node, mark it visited, process it (e.g., record distance), and push all unvisited neighbors onto the stack with updated distance (current distance + edge weight). 4) Continue until all nodes are visited. This ensures we traverse the entire tree. Time complexity is O(n) as each node and edge is visited once. Space complexity is O(n) for stack. This feeds into L2 by providing the traversal mechanism to find paths between nodes. Common mistakes: not using a visited array (though trees are acyclic, it prevents re-visiting), incorrect weight accumulation, and using BFS instead when DFS is more suitable for path accumulation. Real code pattern: def dfs_traversal(start, n, adjacency): visited = [False]*n; stack = [(start, 0)]; while stack: node, dist = stack.pop(); if not visited[node]: visited[node] = True; print(f'Node {node} at distance {dist}'); for neighbor, weight in adjacency[node]: if not visited[neighbor]: stack.append((neighbor, dist + weight)).

examples.trace: Start at node 0: push (0,0). Pop (0,0): visit 0 at dist=0. Push neighbor 1 with dist=0+2=2. Pop (1,2): visit 1 at dist=2. Push neighbors: 2 with dist=2+3=5, 3 with dist=2+1=3. Pop (3,3): visit 3 at dist=3. Pop (2,5): visit 2 at dist=5.
Start at node 1: push (1,0). Pop (1,0): visit 1 at dist=0. Push neighbors: 0 with dist=0+5=5, 2 with dist=0+10=10. Pop (2,10): visit 2 at dist=10. Pop (0,5): visit 0 at dist=5.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Representing a Weighted Tree as Adjacency List
ladders.desc: Create an adjacency list data structure to store a weighted tree for efficient traversal.
ladders.explanation: This fundamental operation involves building a graph representation of the tree, which is necessary for traversal algorithms like DFS. An adjacency list is a list of lists (or dictionaries) where each index corresponds to a node, and the list at that index contains pairs (neighbor, weight) for edges connected to that node. The intuition is that it allows O(1) access to neighbors and their weights, which is efficient for tree traversal. For a tree with n nodes and n-1 edges, we process each edge twice (once for each direction in undirected representation). The algorithm: 1) Initialize an empty list of size n. 2) For each edge [u, v, w] in the input, add v to u's list with weight w, and add u to v's list with weight w (since the tree is undirected). 3) This results in a structure where adjacency[u] = [(v1, w1), (v2, w2), ...]. Time complexity is O(n) to initialize and O(n) to add edges, so overall O(n). Space complexity is O(n) for the list. This feeds into L3 by providing the data structure needed for DFS traversal. Common mistakes: forgetting to add edges in both directions for undirected trees, using adjacency matrix (O(n²) space), and not handling weights correctly. Real code pattern: def build_adjacency(n, edges): adjacency = [[] for _ in range(n)]; for u, v, w in edges: adjacency[u].append((v, w)); adjacency[v].append((u, w)); return adjacency.

examples.trace: Initialize adjacency list of size 4. For edge [0,1,2]: add (1,2) to adjacency[0]; add (0,2) to adjacency[1]. For edge [1,2,3]: add (2,3) to adjacency[1]; add (1,3) to adjacency[2]. For edge [1,3,1]: add (3,1) to adjacency[1]; add (1,1) to adjacency[3].
Initialize adjacency list of size 3. For edge [0,1,5]: add (1,5) to adjacency[0]; add (0,5) to adjacency[1]. For edge [1,2,10]: add (2,10) to adjacency[1]; add (1,10) to adjacency[2].

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Understanding Trees and Edge Weights
ladders.desc: Learn basic tree properties and how edge weights represent distances in graph theory.
ladders.explanation: This is the simplest prerequisite: a tree is an undirected graph with n nodes and exactly n-1 edges, containing no cycles. This means there is exactly one unique path between any two nodes. Edge weights are numerical values assigned to edges, representing concepts like distance, cost, or time. In the context of tree diameter, the weight of a path is the sum of weights along its edges. The intuition is that by understanding these properties, we can reason about paths and distances without cycles complicating things. For example, in a tree with nodes A-B-C and weights 2 and 3, the path from A to C has distance 5 (2+3). This foundation is crucial because it ensures algorithms like DFS will find the unique path. Time and space analysis are not directly applicable here, but understanding these concepts enables building efficient data structures. This feeds into L4 by providing the theoretical basis for representing trees. Common mistakes: confusing trees with general graphs (which may have cycles), misunderstanding that edge weights must be non-negative in distance contexts, and not recognizing the uniqueness of paths. Real code pattern: No code here, but conceptually: tree = acyclic connected graph; edge weight = number on an edge; path distance = sum of weights along a path.

examples.input: Tree with nodes 0-1-2, edges: 0-1 weight=5, 1-2 weight=10
examples.trace: The tree has 3 nodes and 2 edges, no cycles. Path from 0 to 2: 0→1 (weight 5) + 1→2 (weight 10) = total 15.

examples.input: Tree with nodes A-B-C-D, edges: A-B weight=1, B-C weight=2, C-D weight=3
examples.output: Path from A to D has distance 6
examples.trace: Tree has 4 nodes and 3 edges, no cycles. Path from A to D: A→B (1) + B→C (2) + C→D (3) = total 6.

testCases.input: Tree: star with center 0, edges: 0-1 weight=10, 0-2 weight=20, 0-3 weight=30
Tree: line 0-1-2-3-4, edges: 0-1 weight=2, 1-2 weight=3, 2-3 weight=4, 3-4 weight=5
Tree: 0-1-2-3-4-5, edges: 0-1 weight=1, 1-2 weight=2, 2-3 weight=3, 3-4 weight=4, 4-5 weight=5
Tree: 0-1-2-3-4, edges: 0-1 weight=5, 1-2 weight=6, 2-3 weight=7, 3-4 weight=8

approaches.name: Two DFS O(n)
approaches.summary: Perform two depth-first searches: first to find the farthest node from an arbitrary start, then from that node to find the diameter. This works for weighted trees by tracking cumulative edge weights.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Tree Diameter with Weights Using Two DFS
ladders.desc: Find the longest path (by sum of edge weights) between any two nodes in a weighted tree using two DFS traversals.
ladders.explanation: The tree diameter problem with weighted edges requires finding the maximum distance between any two nodes, where distance is the sum of edge weights along the path. The two DFS approach leverages a key property of trees: the farthest node from an arbitrary starting node is always one endpoint of the diameter. First, perform a DFS from any node (typically node 0) to find the farthest node (call it 'endpoint1'). Then, perform a second DFS from endpoint1 to find the farthest node from it (call it 'endpoint2'). The distance between endpoint1 and endpoint2 is the diameter. This works because in a tree, the longest path must be between two leaf nodes (or nodes with degree 1 in the weighted sense), and DFS from any start will reach one end of this path. The algorithm handles weighted edges by accumulating weights during traversal. Time complexity is O(n) as each DFS visits all n nodes once, and space complexity is O(n) for recursion stack and adjacency list storage. Connection to L1: This full solution uses the DFS subroutine from L1 twice. Common mistakes include not handling disconnected graphs (but trees are connected by definition), incorrect weight accumulation, or assuming unweighted edges. Real code pattern: Use adjacency list with (neighbor, weight) pairs, DFS returns farthest node and max distance.

examples.trace: Step 1: Build adjacency list: {0:[(1,2)], 1:[(0,2),(2,3),(3,1)], 2:[(1,3)], 3:[(1,1)]}. Step 2: First DFS from node 0. Distances: node0=0, node1=2, node2=5 (via 0-1-2), node3=3 (via 0-1-3). Farthest node = node2 with distance 5. Step 3: Second DFS from node 2. Distances: node2=0, node1=3, node0=5 (via 2-1-0), node3=4 (via 2-1-3). Farthest node = node0 with distance 5. Diameter = max distance = 5.

Step 1: Adjacency list: {0:[(1,1)], 1:[(0,1),(2,2)], 2:[(1,2),(3,3)], 3:[(2,3),(4,4)], 4:[(3,4)]}. Step 2: First DFS from node 0. Distances: node0=0, node1=1, node2=3, node3=6, node4=10. Farthest node = node4 with distance 10. Step 3: Second DFS from node 4. Distances: node4=0, node3=4, node2=7, node1=9, node0=10. Farthest node = node0 with distance 10. Diameter = 10.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — DFS to Find Farthest Node in Weighted Tree
ladders.desc: Perform a depth-first search from a given start node to find the farthest node and its distance in a weighted tree.
ladders.explanation: This subroutine is the core of the two DFS approach. Given a start node in a weighted tree, DFS traverses all nodes, accumulating edge weights to compute distances from the start. The goal is to return the farthest node (by maximum cumulative weight) and its distance. Intuition: In a tree, DFS explores all paths without cycles, so it correctly computes distances. The algorithm uses recursion or a stack: start with distance 0 at the start node, for each neighbor not visited, add the edge weight to current distance and recurse. Track the maximum distance and corresponding node. Time complexity is O(n) as each node and edge is visited once. Space complexity is O(n) for recursion stack and visited set. Connection to L0: This subroutine is called twice in the full solution—first to find one endpoint, then from that endpoint to find the diameter. Common mistakes include not marking nodes as visited (leading to infinite loops in trees, though trees have no cycles, but still necessary for correctness), or incorrect weight addition. Real code pattern: DFS function takes current node, current distance, visited set, and updates global max_distance and farthest_node.

examples.trace: Step 1: Build adjacency list: {0:[(1,2)], 1:[(0,2),(2,3),(3,1)], 2:[(1,3)], 3:[(1,1)]}. Step 2: DFS from node 0. Visit node0: dist=0. Neighbor node1: dist=0+2=2, recurse. Visit node1: dist=2. Neighbors: node0 visited, node2: dist=2+3=5, recurse. Visit node2: dist=5, no unvisited neighbors, update max: node2 with dist5. Back to node1: node3: dist=2+1=3, recurse. Visit node3: dist=3, update max (5>3, keep node2). Return farthest_node=2, distance=5.

Step 1: Adjacency list: {0:[(1,1)], 1:[(0,1),(2,2)], 2:[(1,2),(3,3)], 3:[(2,3),(4,4)], 4:[(3,4)]}. Step 2: DFS from node 2. Visit node2: dist=0. Neighbor node1: dist=0+2=2, recurse. Visit node1: dist=2. Neighbor node0: dist=2+1=3, recurse. Visit node0: dist=3, update max: node0 with dist3. Back to node1: no more neighbors. Back to node2: neighbor node3: dist=0+3=3, recurse. Visit node3: dist=3. Neighbor node4: dist=3+4=7, recurse. Visit node4: dist=7, update max: node4 with dist7 (7>3). Return farthest_node=4, distance=7. Correction: Actually, from node2, distance to node4 is 2+3+4=9? Wait, edges: 2-1 weight2, 1-0 weight1 gives dist3 from node2 to node0; 2-3 weight3, 3-4 weight4 gives dist7 from node2 to node4. So farthest is node4 with dist7. But in example, output says distance=9, which is incorrect. Let's recalc: From node2, paths: to node0: 2-1-0 = 2+1=3, to node4: 2-3-4 = 3+4=7. So max is 7. I'll correct output to distance=7.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Farthest Node from Start is Diameter Endpoint
ladders.desc: Understand why the farthest node from any arbitrary start node in a tree is always an endpoint of the diameter.
ladders.explanation: This core insight underpins the two DFS approach. In a tree (connected, acyclic graph), consider any node as the start. The farthest node from this start (by edge weight sum) must be one endpoint of the tree's diameter (the longest path). Proof intuition: Suppose the diameter endpoints are A and B. For any start node S, the farthest node from S is either A or B. Why? If S lies on the diameter path, then the farthest point is one of the ends. If S is off the diameter, the path to the farthest node will 'join' the diameter at some point, and due to tree properties, it cannot be longer than going to an endpoint. This relies on the triangle inequality in trees: distances are unique. This property allows us to find one diameter endpoint with one DFS, then from that endpoint, another DFS finds the other endpoint and the diameter length. Connection to L1: This logic justifies why the DFS subroutine can find a diameter endpoint. Common mistakes: Assuming this holds for graphs with cycles (it doesn't) or not considering weighted edges (works with weights as long as distances are additive). Real code pattern: No direct code, but understanding this ensures correct algorithm design.

examples.input: Tree with nodes 0-1-2-3 (linear), weights all 1. Start at node 1.
examples.output: Farthest nodes from node1 are nodes 0 and 3 (distance 2). Diameter endpoints are 0 and 3.
examples.trace: Step 1: Tree: edges (0,1,1), (1,2,1), (2,3,1). Diameter is path 0-3 with length 3. Step 2: Start at node1. Distances: to node0=1, to node2=1, to node3=2. Farthest is node3 (distance 2). Node3 is an endpoint of diameter. Similarly, if start at node2, farthest is node0 or node3 (distance 2), both endpoints.

examples.input: Tree star-shaped: center node0 connected to nodes 1,2,3 with weights 2,3,4.
examples.output: Farthest from node0 is node3 (distance 4). Diameter endpoints are node3 and node1 or node2? Actually, diameter is between two leaves: longest path is via center, e.g., node3 to node2 = 4+3=7, so endpoints node3 and node2.
examples.trace: Step 1: Tree: edges (0,1,2), (0,2,3), (0,3,4). Diameter: between two leaves, say node3 and node2: path 3-0-2, weight 4+3=7. Step 2: Start at node0. Distances: to node1=2, to node2=3, to node3=4. Farthest is node3 (distance 4). Node3 is an endpoint of diameter. Second DFS from node3 would find node2 as farthest with distance 7.

testCases.input: Linear tree 2 nodes, start at node0
testCases.expected: Farthest is node1, endpoint of diameter

testCases.input: Linear tree 3 nodes equal weights, start at middle
testCases.expected: Farthest is either end, endpoint of diameter

testCases.input: Star tree 4 nodes, center node0, start at node0
testCases.expected: Farthest is leaf with max weight, endpoint of diameter

testCases.input: Tree: 0-1-2 with weights 5,10, start at node1
testCases.expected: Farthest is node2, endpoint of diameter

testCases.input: Tree: 0-1-2-3 with weights 1,2,3, start at node2
testCases.expected: Farthest is node3, endpoint of diameter

testCases.input: Tree: 0 connected to 1(weight10) and 2(weight20), start at node1
testCases.expected: Farthest is node2, endpoint of diameter

testCases.input: Tree: binary with root 0, children 1(weight5) and 2(weight5), grandchildren 3(weight5) and 4(weight5), start at root
testCases.expected: Farthest is leaf (e.g., node3), endpoint of diameter

testCases.input: Tree: single node, start at node0
testCases.expected: Farthest is node0, endpoint of diameter (trivial)

testCases.input: Tree: 0-1-2-3-4 linear, weights all 2, start at node2
testCases.expected: Farthest is node0 or node4, endpoint of diameter
Farthest is node2, endpoint of diameter

ladders.title: Building Block — Graph Representation for Weighted Trees
ladders.desc: Learn to represent a weighted tree using adjacency lists for efficient traversal.
ladders.explanation: To perform DFS on a weighted tree, we need an efficient graph representation. An adjacency list is ideal: for each node, store a list of tuples (neighbor, weight). This allows O(1) access to neighbors and their edge weights during traversal. For a tree with n nodes and n-1 edges, this uses O(n) space. Intuition: Trees are sparse graphs, so adjacency lists avoid the O(n^2) space of adjacency matrices. The representation is built from input edges: for each edge (u,v,w), add v to u's list with weight w, and u to v's list (since trees are undirected). Connection to L2: This representation enables the DFS in the core logic. Common mistakes: Forgetting to add both directions for undirected edges, or misstoring weights. Real code pattern: Use a dictionary or list of lists: adj = [[] for _ in range(n)], then for u,v,w in edges: adj[u].append((v,w)); adj[v].append((u,w)).

examples.trace: Step 1: Initialize empty lists for nodes 0,1,2. Step 2: For edge [0,1,5]: add (1,5) to node0's list, add (0,5) to node1's list. Step 3: For edge [1,2,10]: add (2,10) to node1's list, add (1,10) to node2's list. Result: node0: [(1,5)], node1: [(0,5),(2,10)], node2: [(1,10)].

Step 1: Initialize for nodes 0,1,2,3. Step 2: Edge [0,1,2]: add (1,2) to node0, add (0,2) to node1. Step 3: Edge [0,2,3]: add (2,3) to node0, add (0,3) to node2. Step 4: Edge [2,3,4]: add (3,4) to node2, add (2,4) to node3. Result as above.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Depth-First Search (DFS) on Graphs
ladders.desc: Perform a depth-first traversal of a graph to visit all nodes, tracking visited nodes to avoid cycles.
ladders.explanation: DFS is a fundamental graph traversal algorithm that explores as far as possible along each branch before backtracking. For trees, which are acyclic, DFS ensures all nodes are visited exactly once. Intuition: Start at a root node, recursively visit all unvisited neighbors, marking nodes as visited to prevent re-visiting. In trees, this naturally covers all nodes without cycles. The algorithm uses a stack (implicit via recursion or explicit). Time complexity is O(V+E), but for trees, E = V-1, so O(n). Space complexity is O(n) for visited storage and recursion stack. Connection to L3: DFS operates on the adjacency list representation to traverse the tree. Common mistakes: Forgetting to mark nodes as visited (causes infinite recursion in general graphs, though trees have no cycles, but still good practice), or not handling disconnected graphs (trees are connected). Real code pattern: def dfs(node, visited): visited.add(node); for neighbor in adj[node]: if neighbor not in visited: dfs(neighbor, visited).

examples.input: Graph: nodes 0,1,2 with edges 0-1, 1-2. Start DFS at node0.
examples.trace: Step 1: Start at node0, mark visited. Step 2: Neighbors of 0: node1 (unvisited), recurse to node1. Step 3: At node1, mark visited. Neighbors: node0 (visited), node2 (unvisited), recurse to node2. Step 4: At node2, mark visited. Neighbors: node1 (visited), backtrack. Order: 0,1,2.

examples.input: Graph: star with center 0 connected to 1,2,3. Start DFS at node0.
examples.trace: Step 1: Start at node0, mark visited. Step 2: Neighbors: node1, node2, node3. Suppose order: node1 first. Recurse to node1, mark visited. Node1 has neighbor node0 (visited), backtrack. Step 3: Back to node0, next neighbor node2, recurse, mark visited, backtrack. Step 4: Next neighbor node3, recurse, mark visited, backtrack. Order: 0,1,2,3.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Trees as Graphs
ladders.desc: Understand basic properties of trees: connected, acyclic graphs with n nodes and n-1 edges.
ladders.explanation: A tree is a special type of graph that is connected (there is a path between any two nodes) and acyclic (contains no cycles). Key properties: For a tree with n nodes, it has exactly n-1 edges. This makes trees minimally connected—removing any edge disconnects it. Trees are undirected by default in this context. Intuition: Think of a tree as a hierarchy or branching structure, like a family tree or file directory. The acyclic property ensures unique paths between nodes, which is crucial for algorithms like diameter calculation. Connection to L4: DFS on trees leverages these properties to traverse all nodes without worrying about cycles. Common mistakes: Confusing trees with general graphs (which may have cycles or be disconnected), or not noting the n-1 edges property. Real code pattern: No code, but understanding helps in problem constraints: if input has n nodes and n-1 edges, it's likely a tree.

examples.output: This is a tree: connected, no cycles, 3 nodes and 2 edges.
examples.trace: Step 1: Check connectivity: paths exist between all pairs: 0-1, 1-2, and 0-2 via 0-1-2. Step 2: Check cycles: no edges form a loop. Step 3: Count edges: 2 edges, nodes=3, so n-1=2, matches. Thus, it's a tree.

examples.output: This is not a tree: it has a cycle (0-1-2-3-0).
examples.trace: Step 1: Check cycles: edges form a cycle 0-1-2-3-0. Step 2: Count edges: 4 edges, nodes=4, but n-1=3, so too many edges. Thus, not a tree.

approaches.name: DP on Trees O(n)
approaches.summary: Use dynamic programming on the tree with a post-order DFS, where each node computes the longest path through it by combining the top two child subtree depths plus edge weights. The diameter is the maximum over all nodes.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Tree Diameter with Weights using DP on Trees
ladders.desc: Complete solution to find the longest path in a weighted tree using dynamic programming with post-order DFS.
ladders.explanation: The tree diameter problem with weights asks us to find the longest path between any two nodes in a weighted tree, where edge weights represent distances. The DP on Trees approach solves this in O(n) time by treating each node as a potential highest point in the diameter path. The key insight is that for any node, the longest path passing through it consists of the two longest paths from that node to leaves in different subtrees, plus the edge weights to those children. We perform a post-order DFS where each node returns the maximum depth (longest path from that node to any leaf in its subtree). Simultaneously, we compute the candidate diameter at each node by summing the top two child depths plus their edge weights. The overall diameter is the maximum of all these node candidates. Intuition: The diameter must pass through some node as its highest point (farthest from both ends), and at that node, it connects the two deepest subtrees. The post-order ensures child depths are computed before parent. Algorithm: 1. Build adjacency list from edges with weights. 2. Initialize diameter = 0. 3. DFS(node, parent): a. Initialize max_depth = 0, top_two_depths = [0, 0]. b. For each neighbor (child, weight): if child != parent: child_depth = DFS(child, node) + weight. Update max_depth = max(max_depth, child_depth). Update top_two_depths with child_depth (keep two largest). c. diameter = max(diameter, sum of top_two_depths). d. Return max_depth. 4. Start DFS from any node (e.g., 0). 5. Return diameter. Time: O(n) for single DFS traversal. Space: O(n) for recursion stack and adjacency list. Connection to L1: This uses the DFS helper as the key subroutine. Common mistakes: Not handling negative weights (this algorithm assumes non-negative weights for correctness), forgetting to add edge weights when computing child_depth, not resetting top_two_depths for each node, or using pre-order instead of post-order. Real code pattern: def tree_diameter(n, edges): adj = [[] for _ in range(n)]; for u,v,w in edges: adj[u].append((v,w)); adj[v].append((u,w)); diameter = 0; def dfs(node, parent): nonlocal diameter; max_depth = 0; top_two = [0,0]; for child,weight in adj[node]: if child != parent: child_depth = dfs(child, node) + weight; max_depth = max(max_depth, child_depth); if child_depth > top_two[0]: top_two[1] = top_two[0]; top_two[0] = child_depth; elif child_depth > top_two[1]: top_two[1] = child_depth; diameter = max(diameter, sum(top_two)); return max_depth; dfs(0, -1); return diameter.

examples.trace: Tree: 0-1(1), 1-2(2), 1-3(3). DFS from node 0. Node 2: leaf, returns 0; top_two=[0,0]; diameter=0. Node 3: leaf, returns 0; top_two=[0,0]; diameter=0. Node 1: children 2 and 3. child_depth from 2: 0+2=2; from 3: 0+3=3. max_depth=max(2,3)=3. top_two: after 2: [2,0]; after 3: [3,2]. diameter at node 1: sum(top_two)=3+2=5; update diameter=5. Returns max_depth=3 to parent. Node 0: child 1 with weight 1. child_depth from 1: 3+1=4. max_depth=4. top_two: [4,0]. diameter at node 0: sum=4+0=4; diameter remains 5. Returns 4. Final diameter=5. But wait, path 2-1-3 has length 2+3=5, not 6. Let's recalc: edges: 0-1 weight 1, 1-2 weight 2, 1-3 weight 3. Longest path is 2-1-3: 2+3=5. Output should be 5. Correction: diameter=5.

Tree: 0-1(5), 0-2(3), 1-3(2), 1-4(4). DFS from 0. Node 3: leaf, returns 0; diameter=0. Node 4: leaf, returns 0; diameter=0. Node 2: leaf, returns 0; diameter=0. Node 1: children 3 and 4. child_depth from 3: 0+2=2; from 4: 0+4=4. max_depth=4. top_two: after 3: [2,0]; after 4: [4,2]. diameter at node 1: sum=4+2=6; update diameter=6. Returns 4 to parent. Node 0: children 1 and 2. child_depth from 1: 4+5=9; from 2: 0+3=3. max_depth=9. top_two: after 1: [9,0]; after 2: [9,3]. diameter at node 0: sum=9+3=12; update diameter=12. Returns 9. But path 3-1-0-2: 2+5+3=10 or 4-1-0-2: 4+5+3=12? Wait, 4-1-0-2: edges 4-1 weight 4, 1-0 weight 5, 0-2 weight 3: total 4+5+3=12. So diameter=12. But output says 11. Correction: output should be 12. Let's verify: longest path is between node 4 and node 2 via 1 and 0: 4+5+3=12. So output=12.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Post-order DFS to Compute Node Depths
ladders.desc: DFS function that returns the maximum depth from a node to any leaf in its subtree, adding edge weights.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine is the core of the DP approach: a depth-first search that traverses the tree in post-order to compute, for each node, the longest path from that node down to any leaf in its subtree, considering edge weights. The intuition is that to find the diameter passing through a node, we need to know how far each child subtree extends, so we compute these depths bottom-up. The DFS takes a node and its parent to avoid cycles, recursively calls on all children, and for each child, adds the edge weight to the returned depth to get the depth from the current node via that child. It keeps track of the maximum such depth to return to the parent, and also maintains the top two depths to compute the diameter candidate (though that part is in L0). Here, we focus only on returning the max depth. Algorithm: 1. Initialize max_depth = 0. 2. For each neighbor (child, weight): if child != parent: child_depth = DFS(child, node) + weight. max_depth = max(max_depth, child_depth). 3. Return max_depth. Time: O(n) as each node and edge is visited once. Space: O(n) for recursion stack. Connection to L0: This function is called within the main algorithm to compute depths that feed into diameter calculation. Common mistakes: Not adding edge weight, not handling parent check, or using iterative DFS without proper post-order. Real code pattern: def dfs(node, parent): max_depth = 0; for child,weight in adj[node]: if child != parent: depth = dfs(child, node) + weight; max_depth = max(max_depth, depth); return max_depth.

examples.trace: Tree: 0-1(2), 0-2(3). DFS from node 0 with parent -1. Node 1: leaf, returns 0. Node 2: leaf, returns 0. At node 0: child 1: depth = 0+2=2; child 2: depth = 0+3=3. max_depth = max(2,3)=3. Return 3.

Tree: 0-1(1), 1-2(2), 1-3(3). DFS from node 1 with parent -1. Node 0: leaf? Actually 0 has child 1 but parent check avoids cycle, so node 0: no children (since only neighbor is 1 which is parent), returns 0. Node 2: leaf, returns 0. Node 3: leaf, returns 0. At node 1: children: 0 with weight 1: depth=0+1=1; 2 with weight 2: depth=0+2=2; 3 with weight 3: depth=0+3=3. max_depth = max(1,2,3)=3. Return 3.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Combining Top Two Child Depths for Diameter
ladders.desc: At each node, compute the diameter candidate by summing the two largest depths from children plus their edge weights.
ladders.explanation: The central insight of the DP approach is that the longest path passing through a node is formed by the two longest paths from that node to leaves in different subtrees, connected at the node. This works because the diameter is a path between two leaves (or nodes), and if we consider the node as the highest point (most central), the path goes down two different branches. For each node, during DFS, we get depths from each child (child_depth = DFS(child) + edge weight). We keep track of the top two largest child_depths. The sum of these two represents the length of the path that goes through this node and uses these two deepest subtrees. The overall diameter is the maximum such sum over all nodes. Intuition: Imagine the node as a junction; the diameter through it is the sum of the two farthest points reachable via distinct children. This is efficient because we compute it in O(degree) per node during DFS. Algorithm snippet: Initialize top_two = [0,0]. For each child_depth: if child_depth > top_two[0]: top_two[1] = top_two[0]; top_two[0] = child_depth; else if child_depth > top_two[1]: top_two[1] = child_depth. Then candidate = sum(top_two). Time: O(degree) per node, total O(n). Space: O(1) extra per node. Connection to L1: This logic uses the depths returned by the DFS subroutine. Common mistakes: Not initializing top_two for each node, not updating both values correctly, or including the same subtree twice. Real code pattern: top_two = [0,0]; for child_depth in child_depths: if child_depth > top_two[0]: top_two[1] = top_two[0]; top_two[0] = child_depth; elif child_depth > top_two[1]: top_two[1] = child_depth; diameter = max(diameter, sum(top_two)).

examples.trace: Initialize top_two=[0,0]. Process 2: 2>0, so top_two becomes [2,0]. Process 5: 5>2, so top_two becomes [5,2]. Process 3: 3>2 but not >5, so top_two becomes [5,3]. Sum = 5+3=8.
top_two=[0,0]. Process 1: [1,0]. Process 1: 1>1? No, 1>0? Yes, so [1,1]. Process 1: 1>1? No, 1>1? No, so unchanged [1,1]. Process 1: same, unchanged. Sum=1+1=2.

ladders.title: Building Block — Tree Traversal with Parent Tracking
ladders.desc: Traverse a tree using DFS while avoiding cycles by tracking the parent node.
ladders.explanation: In tree problems, we often need to traverse all nodes without revisiting, which requires avoiding cycles. Since a tree is an acyclic connected graph, a simple DFS can traverse it, but we must skip the parent node when exploring neighbors to prevent going back. This is done by passing a parent parameter in the DFS function. Intuition: In a tree, each node (except root) has exactly one parent; by remembering the parent, we ensure we only move to children (neighbors that are not the parent). This allows us to process the tree in a directed manner without explicit visited array. Algorithm: DFS(node, parent): Process node. For each neighbor in adjacency list: if neighbor != parent: DFS(neighbor, node). This works for any starting node. Time: O(n) as each node and edge is visited once. Space: O(n) for recursion stack. Connection to L2: This traversal is used to visit each node and compute child depths. Common mistakes: Forgetting to pass parent, not checking parent condition, or using iterative DFS without proper parent tracking. Real code pattern: def dfs(node, parent): for neighbor in adj[node]: if neighbor != parent: dfs(neighbor, node).

examples.trace: DFS(0, -1): neighbors 1 and 2. Since parent=-1, both are allowed. Go to 1: DFS(1,0): neighbors 0 and 3. Skip 0 (parent), go to 3: DFS(3,1): neighbors 1 only, skip parent, return. Back to 1, return. Back to 0, go to 2: DFS(2,0): neighbors 0 only, skip parent, return. Order: 0,1,3,2.

DFS(1, -1): neighbors 0 and 2. Go to 0: DFS(0,1): neighbor 1 only, skip parent, return. Back to 1, go to 2: DFS(2,1): neighbors 1 and 3. Skip 1, go to 3: DFS(3,2): neighbor 2 only, skip, return. Order: 1,0,2,3.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Compute Maximum of Values in a List
ladders.desc: Find the maximum value in a list of numbers, or the top two maximums.
ladders.explanation: This fundamental operation involves iterating through a list to find the largest value, or in our case, the two largest values. For a single maximum, we initialize a variable to a very small number (or the first element) and update it when we find a larger value. For two maximums, we maintain two variables and update them carefully to ensure we track the largest and second-largest. Intuition: By scanning the list once, we can keep track of the best candidates seen so far. This is efficient with O(n) time and O(1) space. Algorithm for single max: max_val = -inf; for val in list: if val > max_val: max_val = val. For top two: first = second = -inf; for val in list: if val > first: second = first; first = val; else if val > second: second = val. Time: O(n). Space: O(1). Connection to L3: This operation is used during tree traversal to compute max depths from children. Common mistakes: Not initializing properly, missing the else-if condition for second max, or handling empty lists. Real code pattern: first = second = 0; for val in values: if val > first: second = first; first = val; elif val > second: second = val.

examples.trace: Single max: start max=3; 1<3; 4>3 so max=4; 1<4; 5>4 so max=5. Top two: first=0, second=0. 3>0: second=0, first=3. 1>3? No, 1>0? Yes: second=1. 4>3: second=3, first=4. 1>4? No, 1>3? No. 5>4: second=4, first=5. Result: first=5, second=4.

Single max: max=10; 20>10 so max=20; 20=20; 30>20 so max=30. Top two: first=0, second=0. 10>0: second=0, first=10. 20>10: second=10, first=20. 20>20? No, 20>10? Yes: second=20. 30>20: second=20, first=30. Result: first=30, second=20.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Tree as a Graph with No Cycles
ladders.desc: Understand a tree as a connected, acyclic graph with n nodes and n-1 edges.
ladders.explanation: A tree is a fundamental data structure in computer science, defined as an undirected graph that is connected (there is a path between any two nodes) and has no cycles (no closed loops). Key properties: 1. For n nodes, there are exactly n-1 edges. 2. There is exactly one path between any two nodes. 3. Removing any edge disconnects the tree. 4. Adding any edge creates a cycle. Trees are hierarchical, often with a root node, but in graph terms, they can be rooted or unrooted. In algorithm problems, trees are usually given as edges between nodes, and we can treat any node as the root. Intuition: The acyclic property allows simple traversals like DFS without needing a visited array if we track the parent. The connectivity ensures we can reach all nodes from any start. This foundation is crucial for tree DP because we rely on the tree structure to define parent-child relationships and perform bottom-up computations. Example: A tree with 4 nodes might have edges [[0,1],[1,2],[1,3]]; it's connected and has 3 edges (4-1). Common mistakes: Confusing trees with general graphs, not handling the case of a single node, or assuming a root is specified. Real code pattern: To represent a tree, use an adjacency list: adj = [[] for _ in range(n)]; for u,v in edges: adj[u].append(v); adj[v].append(u).

examples.trace: Check: n=3, edges=2 (3-1=2, so okay). Graph is connected: path 0-1-2 connects all. No cycles: no edges like [0,2] that would create a cycle. So it's a tree.
n=4, edges=4 (4-1=3, but has 4 edges, so too many). Also, cycle exists: 0-1-2-3-0 forms a cycle. So not a tree.

testCases.expected: Not a tree (has cycle and disconnected)
